I have started work on implementing the keyword-style, e.g.
```python
fit = Fit(new, x=xdata, y=ydata, data=zdata) 
```
Named dependent variables are not supported yet, for the moment I will stick to scalar valued functions whose data *has* to be supplied via the ```data``` keyword.

Positional arguments will also be supported. The order is given as model, variables in alphabetical order, data, optional arguments for the ```Fit``` object at hand, e.g. sigma. So in the above line, the following would be equivalent:
```python
fit = Fit(new, xdata, ydata, zdata) 
```
This guarantees backwards compatibility in the case of 1 scalar to 1 scalar functions, which will be most use cases.

Of course the recommended way is by using keyword arguments, as this increases readability enormously. This will be the only way thought in the docs.

By the way, I posted a new suggestion for vector valued functions in #15. This new syntax might be the best to date.

Okay, that makes sense. pysrp & csrp were not originally designed to
interoperate with other SRP implementations. So far as I know, there is no
official "this is the standard way to implement SRP in general" but
apparently there are several libraries that adhere to the SSL-defined
implementation approach (not sure which ones those are but there's
definitely more than one). There's an rfc5054_compat branch in both the
pysrp & csrp git repositories that match the RFC5054 implementation
requirements. Several people have had good luck interoperating with other
implementations when using that branch so it's probably what you're looking
for. At some point I'll probably merge that branch into the mainline and
make it the default implementation.

Tom

I think the sentence you quoted sounds a bit weird, indeed. I think the example that follows the sentence kind of explains it, but I would welcome a PR to improve the docs.

So what happens is that the probabilities among the classifiers are averaged, not the probabilities within a classifier. For example, if we have 4 classifiers for the 3-class iris dataset, not-averaging (i.e., stacking) would lead to 12 meta feature columns (each classifier would return a probability for each of the 3 classes). If we set `average_probas=True`, we get only 3 meta-feature columns; here, all class 1 probabilities (4, because we have 4 classifiers) get averaged, all class 2 probas get averaged, and all class 3 probas get averaged.

On Linux, zap-cli currently looks for a file named `zap.sh` rather than just `zap` in the ZAP path directory, to match what exists when you unpack the ZAP archive.

Was ZAP installed via a package manager? If so, it looks like `/usr/local/bin/zap` just [forwards commands to `/usr/share/zaproxy/zap.sh`](https://github.com/zaproxy/zaproxy/blob/5644e28aaaf/build/debian/zap). So, you could try pointing --zap-path to `/usr/share/zaproxy`. i.e.
```
zap-cli --zap-path /usr/share/zaproxy --api-key 12345 quick-scan --self-contained -o '-config api.key=12345' -s xss http://127.0.0.1/
```

Does that work for you?

It just occurred to me that the tensor_conv operations are asynchronous (obviously), and that the way the code was written didn't ensure the cuda_data_void_ptrs work buffer handles existed for the entire duration that cuDNN might be using them.  Meaning that, for instance, if a tensor_conv was running, and then another tensor_conv started to run but needed bigger work buffers then it would deallocate the buffer right under the previous, but still running, tensor_conv.  

I'm not sure why this wasn't happening during any of the tests.  I guess layers near the input probably use bigger workspaces most of the time so we were getting lucky.  Anyway, I just pushed a fix for this.  Let me know if you think I missed something important about it or it's not right.  It's passing my tests though and seems like a straightforward fix.

(a, b), *c = [(1,2), 3, 4]

I played with the `resources` library long ago and I remember there was some quirk.

You are right, `ProcessExpiredError` is raised when a process dies unexpectedly but the `resources` library actually raises a `MemoryError` which will be caught and delivered to the `Pool` like any regular Python exception. Pebble does not kill processes if Python exceptions are triggered, it just reports them to the main loop.

The `MemoryError` will interrupt the running routine without actually crashing the process.
I believe this is fine as long as the job logic does not leak some resource such as threads etc.  Otherwise, `ulimit` might be a better option.

Regarding the last point it boils down to what I said in the previous comment: `you start walking on a OS-specific domain`

@JTKBowers Thanks for reporting this bug. It seems that NLPIR occasionally likes to mix up it's return value structure. Note the difference in the log messages between the two function calls:
```python
>>> pynlpir.get_key_words('我们都是美国人。', weighted=True)
DEBUG:pynlpir:Searching for up to 50 weighted key words in: 我们都是美国人。.
DEBUG:pynlpir:Finished key word search: 美国/nsf/0.01/1#.
DEBUG:pynlpir:Formatting key word search results.
DEBUG:pynlpir:Key words formatted: [('美国', 0.01)].
[('美国', 0.01)]
>>> pynlpir.get_key_words('我们很好,你呢', weighted=True)
DEBUG:pynlpir:Searching for up to 50 weighted key words in: 我们很好,你呢.
DEBUG:pynlpir:Finished key word search: 我们/rr/-1.00.
DEBUG:pynlpir:Formatting key word search results.
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "pynlpir/__init__.py", line 219, in get_key_words
    word, pos, weight, count = w.split('/')
ValueError: need more than 3 values to unpack
```

Normally NLPIR's `GetKeyWords` function returns key words like this:
```
美国/nsf/0.01/1
```
This tells us the word, part of speech, weight, and number of occurrences. However, with your sentence, the following is returned:
```
我们/rr/-1.00
```
So, not only is the weight negative, but there is no value that indicates the number of occurrences. The latter problem is what is causing the error message you're seeing.

I think this bug (which seems to be more on NLPIR's side) will force us to put an extra conditional in the PyNLPIR `get_key_words()` function to account for this problem.

I can't figure out why the weight is negative or why the word count is not included in the return. That would be helpful information to have, if anyone can figure it out. Either way, I'll commit a fix for this shortly.

If multiple tiles from opposed sides of projection bounds are fed into ``create_mosaic()`` function, an output array is being initialized that covers wall-to-wall the projection area and thus causes a ``MemoryError``.

This happens mainly on the map edge using the ``baselevel`` interpolation functionality together with ``pixelbuffer`` but can potentially also be caused by calling the ``get_raw_output()`` function (e.g. through ``mapchete serve`` or a direct API call).

The best solution would be updating ``create_mosaic`` so that it can detect antimeridian crossing and avoiding initialization of a huge array though it is not yet clear whether this way is feasible.

An alternative would be avoiding this use case completely which could be complicated as well.

Postponing issue to a later milestone but creating a new issue handling ``baselevel`` errors caused by this. (#74)

This is the bug:

```
class Descriptor:
    def __get__(self, obj, objtype):
        return 'foo'
    
    def __delete__(self, obj):
        print('del', obj)

class X:
    x = Descriptor()

instance = X()
instance.__dict__['x'] = 'bar'
instance.x  # 'foo'

import inspect
inspect.getattr_static(instance, 'x')  # 'bar', should return a `Descriptor` instance
```

getattr_static only checks if `__set__` is part of the descriptor and not if either `__set__` or `__delete__` are set. (Implementation detail: Both of these methods set the same slot called `tp_descr_set` in CPython).

```
#if (_WIN32_WINNT >= 0x0601)  // Windows 7
```

...if the exe/wheel is compiled with Windows 7 the resulting exe/wheel will not be compatible with win < 7 (e.g. Vista). On Vista, the wheel / exe will "believe" it's running on Windows 7 when it's not. As such you should use some other strategy to determine the Windows version **at runtime** (a Windows API call).

For extra training dataset, the Thai National Corpus can be used with respect to a specific phrase.
It's a resource aimed for linguists, so no download or API access.
We can do search though its online query, or direct URL access like this:

www.arts.chula.ac.th/~ling/TNCII/x3.php?p=รู้&w2=แบบ&wl=0&wr=1

It will look for 2 words "รู้" and "แบบ" where the second word appear in a window-left=0, windown-right=1. It means "แบบ" just follow "รู้", but we can search using a longer distance context if we wish.

Or you can just use google to find "รู้แบบ", but TNC is designed to be "balanced" which should give us a more representative sample.

The docs refer to committing offsets *during* a rebalance. Your error comes
when you miss the rebalance entirely and commit after a new group is formed
without your consumer in it.
On Feb 10, 2016 6:48 AM, "Pehat" <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> I have a consumer reading a single partition. The code is:
>
> for message in self.consumer:
>     result = make_love(message)  # up to 10 seconds; it's a long time, believe me!
>     make_baby(result)
>     try:
>         self.consumer.commit()
>     except IllegalGenerationError:
>         logging.error('Illegal generation caught! Skipping it, huh')
>
> As far as I know, it goes as follows:
> 1) Consumer picks the next message from the queue.
> 2) Heavy processing routine is executed.
> 3) GroupCoordintor performs rebalancing, thus incrementing the current
> generation number.
> 4) A new object is created using result as an argument.
> 5) Consumer commits the offset of the successfully processed message.
> 6) GroupCoordinator responses with IllegalGenerationError because while
> consumer has been processing the task, actual generation number has
> incremented so that the consumer becomes illegal.
> 7) Consumer reconnects, fetches the very message it has processed already
> (but failed to commit), resulting in spawning twin objects (undesired
> behaviour).
>
> Meanwhile, the docs
> <https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/KAFKA/Kafka+0.9+Consumer+Rewrite+Design#Kafka0.9ConsumerRewriteDesign-Consumer>
> say the following:
>
> The consumer connects to the co-ordinator and sends a HeartbeatRequest. If
> an IllegalGeneration error code is returned in the HeartbeatResponse, it
> indicates that the co-ordinator has initiated a rebalance. The consumer
> then stops fetching data, *commits offsets* and sends a JoinGroupRequest
> to it's co-ordinator broker. In the JoinGroupResponse, it receives the list
> of topic partitions that it should own and the new generation id for it's
> group. At this time, group management is done and the consumer starts
> fetching data and (optionally) committing offsets for the list of
> partitions it owns.
>
> Of course, I can commit() at first and make_baby() thereafter, but in
> case of make_baby() failure it would be at-most-once processing, so it's
> an undesired behavior, too.
>
> The question is: should the consumer be able to successfully commit its
> offsets if it gets an IllegalGenerationError? If not, how can I perform
> the exactly-once semantics?
>

@RyanBalfanz your comment seems to have disappeared from github, so I'm copying here for reference:

> @evansd Should we {% load static %} or {% load static from staticfiles %} as in the docs?
>
> It seems like different suggestions exists from different authorities -- If one doesn't read the WhiteNoise Docs and just follows Django's static files how-to and Heroku's django-assets article they wouldn't know to prefer the latter. It's a bit unclear IMO.

Sorry, that was a typo. It should be `{% load staticfiles %}` or `{% load static from staticfiles %}`. Both do exactly the same thing but the latter is a bit more explicit about which tag is being imported.

It's a confusing feature of Django that the `static` tag behaves differently depending on whether you've loaded the `staticfiles` tag library or not. But for backwards compatibility reasons that isn't going to change so we just have to work with it.

I think the Heroku docs should be amended to mention that you should load the `staticfiles` library in your templates. Do you mind making that suggestion to them? There's a feedback form on the docs page.

There should be no issues with round-tripping binary data, but it's awkward because *Python* and *Redis* independently do weird things.

The tests aren't great, but https://github.com/josiahcarlson/rom/blob/master/test/test_rom.py#L1422 shows binary round-tripping in Python 3.x and Python 2.x using the `rom.String` column.

If you want to compress json data before storing it in the column, you can look into how rom round-trips binary string data, then just do your conversions before/after that binary is round-tripped.

After you update the `innerHTML` of the element, you will need to call the tablesorter plugin.

```js
$('#data')
  .html(response)
  .tablesorter({ /* options */ });
```

If the table is populated server-side then you'll need to initialize tablesorter when the document is ready  (see the [getting started](https://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#Getting-Started) section.

If you continue to alter the table content, then initialize tablesorter first, then update the `tbody` and trigger "update" event on the table - you can follow the example in [this demo](https://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/example-ajax.html).

[Here is a demo](https://jsfiddle.net/Mottie/bbxxomhx/) that initializes an in-place table showing nearly all the options.

Well... that's a good question :-)  So, how about, we focus on the convolutional layer?  That is the main bottleneck.  And rather than optimizing the conv layer in each library one by one, how about we switch all the libraries to use @naibaf7 's [greentea convolutional layer](https://github.com/BVLC/caffe/issues/4155)?  Are you coming mostly from a 'for fun' angle? Or for a university/research project? Or as part of your job?  If it is for academic research, or as part of your job, you might be able to get a very quick win by doing:

- run benchmarks across all of deepcl, cltorch, cl-caffe now, record those results somewhere
- migrate cltorch, and possibly also deepcl, to use greentea convolutions
- (hopefully) show a speed boost
- ... and then start looking into how you can further optimize greentea convolutions :-)

It will be easier to migrate cltorch to use greentea convolutions, than deepcl.  Since deepcl needs to run on windows, and be compilable using msvc2008 and msvc2010, so it can work with python 2.7 and python 3.4.

Alternatively, you can just look at making very fast convolutions in greentea, and I can handle migrating cltorch, and possibly also deepcl, onto greentea convolutional layer.

This got fixed in a roundabout way. First, we changed the kinds of all the fortpy variables to be `fdp`, `fsp`, etc. Any variables declared and used exclusively by fortpy in the drivers are declared using those types as well (which are made public in `fortpy.f90` and are thus in the scope of the driver). Next, I added _type_ dependency checking to the driver generation. If a variable needs to be declared with kind `foo`, then fortpy searches all the modules in its libraries to find the public definition of the kind `foo` and adds a use statement so that module's scope was imported.

The previous problem was caused because `num_types.f90` was not being imported explicitly, so the drivers were relying on the definitions of the kinds in `fortpy.f90`. If a developer wants to use one of the fortpy routines, they will make sure that the kinds they declare match the definitions of `fdp` and so on.

Regarding the `callback`. Personally I would like to leave it in there. All parameter are optional, so if you decide to assign the callback later nothing prevents you from doing that. Hi, I'm glad you're finding it useful, and thanks for reaching out with your question!

The reason you're seeing issues is that templating must be explicitly declared for elements (it is not done automatically, for a couple reasons). 

I've got a good example of this in the [content-test](https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/blob/master/pyresttest/content-test.yaml#L23).    So, for your case, the final test will look like: 

```yaml
- test:
    - name: "Logout"
    - url: "/user/logout?token=$token"
    - method: 'POST'
    - headers: {template: {Authorization: "Bearer $token"}}
```
One note: for headers, if you declare them as using a template, *all* values in the header map will be templated (header names and values), so if you're using $ signs in there, make sure to escape them with two $ signs (i.e. $$escaped).

```python
# uinput_test.py
from evdev import UInput, ecodes

ui = UInput()

for key in 'hello':
    ui.write(ecodes.EV_KEY, ecodes.ecodes['KEY_%s' % key.upper()], 1)
    ui.write(ecodes.EV_KEY, ecodes.ecodes['KEY_%s' % key.upper()], 0)

ui.syn()
input('Press enter to exit ... ')
ui.close()
```

Press enter to exit ... hello

I'd like to add this functionality.  I probably won't get to this in the near future though.

Here's how to do this (in case some helpful awesome person wants to make a pull-request):
You can quite easily take an affine transformation of lines, quadratics, and cubics by simply transforming the coefficients.  E.g.,
 
	p = parse_path("m 28.35,0 c 0,15.65621 -12.69107,28.3485 -28.34728,28.35")
	A = np.array([[-1e-4, -1], [1, -1e-4]])
	b = np.array([670.77002, 132.92999])

	# these convert between real 2-vectors and complex numbers
	xy = lambda z: np.array([z.real, z.imag])
	z = lambda xy: xy[0] + 1j*xy[1]


	transformed_path = Path(*[bpoints2bezier([z(np.dot(xy(pt),A) + b)
								for pt in seg.bpoints()]) 
									for seg in p])

This should work (I didn't test it, but just wanted to give the idea) for everything by path's containing Arc objects.  Arc objects shouldn't be too hard to deal with either (see Arc.rotated() and Arc.translated()).
... it might be nice to just add a Path.transformed() method to deal with affine transformations in general.

I think that your problem could be easily solved by placing all the entry
points for your skins in the REQUIRE_BASE_URL directory. This is something
RequireJS generally recommends, as it makes the configuration much easier.
Something like:


   - js/
      - default.js
      - default/
         - scss/
         - other-default-skin-file.js
      - dark.js
      - dark/
         - scss/
         - other-dark-skin-file.js

It's a bit less neat, but it should solve your issues with unduly
complicating django-require.

> <script> tags in the header part for that, one for loading require.js
> itself, and then the other to load common.js. This would load these two
> scripts before everything else, since they would be in the header at the
> top level. The point of this is, this way I won't need to require
> common.js in each inline require tag, as suggested here
> <http://requirejs.org/docs/api.html#data-main>.
>
> So in development mode, my new tag would produce a tag something like this:
>
> <script src="/static/bower_components/requirejs/require.js"></script>
> <script src="/static/skins/default/js/common.js"></script>
>
> Of course for this approach, I'd have to set baseUrl in the config, but
> that's something I can live with. Since at collectstatic I'd compile
> everything together in one file with almond, this wouldn't be an issue
> there.
> ------------------------------
>
> To sum up:
>
>    - Adding entry point filenames and relative paths to the currently
>    working tag would break the existing functionality for most of the people
>    - The new tag would produce a slightly different HTML output in
>    development mode, for which a different approach is needed.
>
> I'm really open for a discussion on this, but keep in mind I've been
> playing around with this in the last several days trying to find a way, and
> to me this seems the only plausible solution.
>

Hey @pior we chatted in #pyramid already (I'm raydeo). I'll add a note here just incase it helps someone else. We discussed two options.

1) Pyramid supports natively overriding most config directives based on its commit-cycle. So you can simply `config.commit()` and then add a new service for that `(iface, context, name)` combo. This requires access to the config object though which only works well in unit tests.

2) You can use an ini setting or something to dispatch your code to register different services at config time.

```python
if asbool(config.registry.settings.get('use_dummy_services', False)):
    register_dummy_services(config)
else:
    register_default_services(config)
```

This is brute force but clean and you can add extra config settings if you want more fine-grained knobs on which services you are replacing.

Hi @vbrchn!

It's of course not a dumb question, I'll try to explain it to you. Basically Django ORM is created for relational databases and Cassandra is very specific noSQL database. It's nearly impossible to plug into Django ORM and execute efficient queries to Cassandra. Also modeling data for Cassandra is very different from we're all used to. That's why I decided to use cassandra-driver and Cqlengine as object mapper, which is maintained by DataStax, and very well documented.

This way you can use Django apps you're used to (auth, admin, contenttypes, etc.) along with apps dedicated to cassandra for better performance. Sometimes you don't need to use Django apps and then (and only then) use django-cassandra-engine as default (primary) database backend.

If you have further questions don't hesitate to ask.

Small concern about your fix though, previous code used latin-1 encoding to keep byte values as is, your valid CSV solution use UTF-8. It will support Unicode characters but will change binary key names. 

Library user have a way to control desired byte/text manipulation by setting the string escape parameter. `MemoryCallback.emit_record()` take care of this. With previous code, an attempt to use UTF-8 parsing would have fail, but the default option work for all cases. 
A possible resolution for this is to add code to allow `MemoryCallback.__init__()` to set stream encoding, something like:
`self._stream.set_encoding('utf8' if self._escape == encodehelpers.STRING_ESCAPE_UTF8 else 'latin-1')`

Can you please address this issue in this PR?

No, there isn't a way to control that. We process the entries in the order in which they are *stored* in the rdb file. The parser is stateless, it is designed to use as little memory as possible during parsing. Returning records in a different order will require holding all elements in memory, and that isn't a good idea.

You can easily solve this by writing a new class that extends `RdbCallback`. Accumulate records in `zadd`, in `end_sorted_set` method sort all the records and apply your business logic.

Do you think you might have forgotten to write `import autograd.numpy as np`?

That looks like an error caused by passing an Autograd-boxed value to a raw numpy kernel, instead of going through the thin wrapper provided by `autograd.numpy` primitives (which just unbox inputs and rebox outputs, recording the operation into a graph that is later used to propagate gradient information).

On the current master commit, I get the same error as you when I don't use `import autograd.numpy as np` and instead do `import numpy as np`. However, there are a couple more problems you'll run into when you fix the import. For one, `grad` only applies to scalar-output functions, and while it won't raise an error for vector- or matrix-output functions, it does something which might be surprising. Another is that `np.linalg.eigh` actually returns a pair (a vector of eigenvalues and a matrix of eigenvectors), so even `jacobian` won't like that kind of output. However, this code works for me:

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad, jacobian

sym = lambda X: (X + X.T) / 2.
X = sym(np.random.randn(2, 2))
print grad(lambda X: np.linalg.eigh(X)[1])(X)
print grad(lambda X: np.sum(np.linalg.eigh(X)[1]))(X)  # actually does the same thing
```

There are a lot of places where `eigh` isn't differentiable, including the identity matrix, so watch out for nans!

You are correct about the `required` trait. One thing to consider, some services will have conditionally required elements, such that field b is required when field a is present. Another funny one is exclusively required fields, such that a or b, but not both must be set. These types of conditions are not described or tracked by the required trait.

The list of fields that must be set in a json document depend mostly on the context. The majority of fields though are optional. For example, when you are looking in the dictionary of shapes, the type field is always set. If the shape is a scalar shape, this is the only field that must be set. If you are looking at a structure shape then the members dictionary must be set. The required list is an optional trait, do is documentation, etc.

I have a work-in-progress, and very much not yet complete JSON schema for the API models here:
https://github.com/awslabs/aws-model-validators/blob/master/schemas/api_v2.json

I tried a bunch of different approaches. Turned out there is no "easy" way to create a collection of object files using CUDA cmake macros. There might be some ways of doing this, but I think it would be too sophisticated ways nobody can maintain.

So, here is a list of approaches I can think of:
1. Leave the CUDA related part as it is in this patch.
2. Change to build static library only by default when CUDA backend is specified. Build shared library if `BUILD_SHARED_LIBS=ON` is specified for those who want to build the shared library. (The latter case, users need to set LD_LIBRARY_PATH or DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH properly)
3. Build static and shared libraries by compiling twice, then link the static library when generating "_dynet.so".

I'm wondering how DyNet is used in Linux environment. If most users don't care about installing the shared libraries under / (e.g., /usr/lib, /usr/local), the first approach sounds better. Otherwise, the second approach seems good to me.

The Python precedent I'd cite here is `pip-tools`, which makes a very clear distinction between "update the lock file from the input file" (`pip-compile`) and "update the current environment to exactly match the lock file" (`pip-sync`, which upgrades, downgrades, installs, and uninstalls things as needed to make the environment match the specification). This is a good model, since it clearly separates the dependency management process into three phases:

* define what you want/need (recorded in `Pipfile`)
* resolve the full dependency tree (recorded in `Pipfile.lock`)
* make a particular environment match the specification (shown by `pipenv graph`)

Right now, `pipenv` doesn't model that management process clearly at all - `pipenv install` and `pipenv uninstall` cover all three aspects (with a grab bag of arcane switches to turn different substeps on and off), and only the middle step is clearly exposed as its own subcommand (`pipenv lock`).

With `pipenv sync` added, then using `pipenv install` and `pipenv uninstall` would become entirely optional: you'd be free to instead just always edit `Pipfile` directly, use `pipenv lock` to update the lock file, then `pipenv sync` to get an environment to match that.

And then the later steps in #1255 would aim to ensure behavioural consistency by having `install` and `uninstall` actually run both `lock` and `sync` in order to make the change they make take effect locally.

@stewartadam raises a great point. I believe that API and mobile apps are really just webapps under the hood, but logic apps aren't. Are we going to have "logicapp" and "webapp" as top level resources (my vote is an emphatic no).

You could also have a structure with "app" as the root and place "service-plan" as a subgroup inside, while the commands to create and manage apps would be at this basic "app" level. This is a bit of an inversion from the norm, but I think it could be justified based on (1) the fact that the appservice team wants the concept of "app service plans" to be essentially transparent, and (2) the fact that we can (and it has been recommended) that we create the service plan when we create the webapp if it doesn't already exist.

I'm guessing because you initialize the ctable with zero length columns,
bcolz will pick a small chunk length, which will cause slowness. Suggest to
try providing the expectedlen=n argument when creating the ctable where n
is the total number of rows you expect in the final table. Alternatively
you could try manually setting chunk length by providing chunklen=m where m
is a parameter you can tune.

Hi,
I hit the same problem, on CentOS 7. It seems to be related to curl issues especially when behind a company proxy (ours was ZScaler), as the certificate from the the remote site is signed by your companies certificate which is not known by curl. I resolved this issue, by explicitly telling curl to use the correct certificate, so in my case that was

`export CURL_CA_BUNDLE=/etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt`

as I'd already imported the ZScaler certificate with `update-ca-trust`

should be the default anyway, but it worked and "`conda update conda`" worked afterwards

Probably best to move this issue to the conda/conda-build repo :)

BTW, you don't have to make files writable to delete them.  The ability to remove a file is actually controlled by the *directory* permissions.  For example

```
$ touch test-file
$ chmod -w test-file
$ python
Python 3.5.2 (default, Sep 15 2016, 07:38:42)
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 7.3.0 (clang-703.0.31)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.

>>> import os
>>> os.unlink('test-file')
>>> f = open('test-file', 'w')
>>>

```
cd %MyDocuments%
gedit MyTODOList.txt

Error: cannot find MyTODOList.txt
```

You can always start a Jenkins job to test that your pull request (PR) does not break anything. 

To do so:
- go to http://jenkins.plone.org
- log in with your Github user
- click on the [Pull Request 5.1 job](http://jenkins.plone.org/job/pull-request-5.1/)
- click on the huge menu link ["Build with Parameters"](http://jenkins.plone.org/job/pull-request-5.1/build?delay=0sec)
- paste the pull request URL into the text field (if multiple pull requests need to be combined in one run, then one PR URL per line)
- click on **Build**

Jenkins reports back to the pull request(s) on Github in the status box at the bottom.

In Plone we value a lot our testing suite, so we usually never merge pull requests if they have not been tested by Jenkins first.

I used implementation of ResNet-18 from resnet.py as a baseline and modified it in the following ways

2) I set num_group = num_filter for each 3x3 convolutional layer starting from 2a.

@jenisys Could you please help me with formatters API?
It seems that *all* ``step()`` callbacks are called before actual step execution. And before execution it calls ``match()``, but match does not have link to step it actually matched. So, there is no way to say which step Behave is going to execute now.

Here is an example

```

Feature: Spam

  Scenario: Eggs
    Given I love BDD
    Then I use Behave

####
from behave import *

@given("I love BDD")
def step_impl(context):
    print("I love BDD executed")

@then("I use Behave")
def step_impl(context):
    print("I use behave executed")


####
from behave import runner, configuration
from behave.formatter import _registry
from behave.formatter.base import Formatter


class MyFormatter(Formatter):

    def feature(self, feature):
        print "Reported Feature" + str(feature)

    def scenario(self, scenario):
        print "Reported Scenario" + str(scenario)

    def step(self, step):
        print "Reported step" + str(step)

    def match(self, match):
        print "Reported match" + str(match)

    def result(self, step_result):
        print "Reported result" + str(step_result)


_registry.register_as("MyFormatter", MyFormatter)
cfg = configuration.Configuration()
cfg.log_capture = False
cfg.stdout_capture = False
cfg.format = ["MyFormatter"]
runner.Runner(cfg).run()
```

Here is my output

```
Reported Feature<Feature "Spam": 1 scenario(s)>
Reported Scenario<Scenario "Eggs">
Reported step<given "I love BDD"> # First step reported but NOT executed nor mateched yet
Reported step<then "I use Behave"> # Second step reported but NOT executed nor mateched yet
Reported match<Match step_impl, steps\steps.py:5> # Function will be executed now, but which step it belongs to?
I love BDD executed
Reported result<given "I love BDD">
Reported match<Match step_impl, steps\steps.py:10>
I use behave executed
Reported result<then "I use Behave">
```

PyCharm requires following sequence of envents:

* Feature started
* Scenario started
* Step1 started
* Step1 finished
* Step2 started
* Step2 finished
* Feature finished # this one 
* Scenario finished # and this one are very useful, but something we can live without


Since ``match()`` is called by step, we may have ``match(match, step)`` signature (and use ``formatter.match(match, self)`` to call it) but that may break API. We may also add ``step`` field to ``Match`` class and it seems to be safe. If you have plans on 1.2.6 I can send you PR.
Or do we have some other approach to solve this?

`lxml.etree.fromstring()` keeps comment nodes seperate. `lxml.html.fromstring()` does not.
I wasn't able to [tell the HTML parser](http://lxml.de/api/lxml.etree.HTMLParser-class.html) to keep comment nodes, but there may be something.

```
>>> t = '''<script type="text/javascript"> 
... <!-- comment in here -->
... alert("oi");
... </script>'''

>>> import lxml.html
>>> doc = lxml.html.fromstring(t)
>>> doc.xpath('//script/descendant::node()')
' \n<!-- comment in here -->\nalert("oi");\n'

>>> import lxml.etree
>>> doc = lxml.etree.fromstring(t)
>>> doc.xpath('//script/descendant::node()')
[' \n', <!-- comment in here -->, '\nalert("oi");\n']
```

And I don't know if it's lxml or libxml2 that does this aggregation of comment nodes with text nodes.

In parsel, one can use `type="xml"` as a workaround:

```
>>> s = parsel.Selector(text=unicode(t), type="xml")
>>> s.xpath('//script/comment()').extract()
[u'<!-- comment in here -->']
>>> s.xpath('//script/text()').extract()
[u'\n', u'\nalert("oi");\n']
```

Further small changes:

* `Frame.flags` is now `Frame.more`, and is a bool rather than a bitfield.  There is only one flag right now, and it doesn't make a lot of sense to support flags that don't exist yet (despite having existed in the past in 3.0 and 4.0).

* default `ZMQStream.send()` to `copy=True` instead of `copy=False`.  It doesn't make sense for its defaults to be different, and can actually cause problems in some edge cases (see #173).

One last thing I am considering before finishing this up:  Full removal of the `prefix` arg on send_multipart.  This was added to support ZMQ_LABEL functionality, which has since been removed.  Right now, all it does is prepend a second the prefix frames to the message.  Perhaps it is more sensible to wait until the feature is reintroduced before restoring the argument, because it is not guaranteed that the prefix arg will even make sense when it comes back.  I obviously wouldn't remove it if anyone is using it, but I seriously doubt that this is the case.

It just replaces the `(inputs - mean) / std * gamma + beta` by a composite Op, which saves memory at the cost of performance. At my intervention it can be disabled by passing `mode="high_mem"`, though, and then it's the same as not using it.

> Someone was talking in a previous conversation about after training to have the option to collect for inference the mean/std on minibatches from the full set. Would that be an option?

To do it as in the paper, you need to collect those statistics over the training set one layer at a time, that's something you'll need to do outside the layer (and you may even want to use double precision accumulators for that). We can document that in a Recipe, but I don't think the layer can help with that.

We could discuss adding some more keyword arguments to `get_output_for()`, though. Right now `deterministic=False` uses the minibatch-dependent statistics and updates the running means, and `deterministic=True` uses the running means. Actually, the former is not really indeterministic, it just happens to match with how we usually obtain the training and test predictions. We could have some extra keyword arguments like `batch_norm_update_averages` and `batch_norm_use_averages` to override the default behaviour.

Two other thoughts:

1. In #429, we thought about allowing users to pass an `updates` dictionary to `get_output`/`get_output_for` where layers can register additional updates. That's needed for recurrent layers involving nondeterminism, and we could use it for the `BatchNormLayer` as well, instead of the `default_updates` trick. That's something we can change later, though (as long as we change it before releasing Lasagne 0.2).
2. After training, it's possible to merge mean/std into gamma/beta, to make evaluation a little faster. Shall we add some support for that or leave it up to the user? Actually, gamma/beta can often even be merged into the weights of the previous layer...

Yes, it is a bug in Augeas, but it's fixed in version 1.4.0: https://github.com/hercules-team/augeas/blob/release-1.5.0/NEWS#L149-L151

The aug_match call in ApacheConfigurator's `get_virtual_hosts` returns a list of paths which in older versions aren't escaped, so they're not safe to pass back into aug_match in `_create_vhost`. It should work on 1.4.0 or higher.

That said, I'm unsure if some of the certbot code would handle it OK, namely `get_file_path` which assumes it can just strip off the `/files` prefix and `/VirtualHost` suffix to get the filesystem path from the Augeas path. It should probably instead strip the suffix only, then call `aug.aug_get("/augeas%s/path" % vhost_path)` to get the real filesystem path.  Some more testing might be needed!

This PR is needed due to a bug I introduced with the combination of #4970 and #4971. Despite being written by me at similar times, those PRs were written separately and this patch is needed when the PRs are combined together.

The need for this can be seen by playing with the test written here on CentOS 6. You can run a test farm test, but you can also just run it manually by running `yum install epel-release -y`, cloning this repo to a directory named `letsencrypt`, checking out the code you want to test, and running this script from the directory containing the `letsencrypt` directory.

Without these changes to `letsencrypt-auto`, [this test](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/5392/files#diff-83d32e155b116db8dfad1d6177025c9eR73) will fail. What happens on this line is `cb-auto` upgrades from 0.5.0 to the version in `letsencrypt-auto-source` and "phase 2" of that script runs. (This is accomplished by the old version of the script providing `--le-auto-phase2` on the command line.) When phase 2 runs, we check if there are new OS dependencies that need to be installed before installing the latest version of Certbot. We determine this by looking at files in the existing Certbot installation to see what version of our dependencies we installed.

The problem that occurs is we only check the new venv location in `/opt` and not the old location in `~/.local/share/letsencrypt`. (In #4971 I added this check here but in #4970  I added the need to check a second location.) As a result, we incorrectly think we just installed OS dependencies as this is what happens at the beginning of "phase 1" if there is no existing installation and try to install Certbot. This installation fails because we try to use Python 3 which hasn't been installed yet and the tests exit with an error saying we shouldn't have tried to upgrade. Instead, we should have printed a message about needing to install new packages to upgrade and continue running the version of Certbot installed in the existing venv.

Hopefully this helps the reviewer. Feel free to ping me however makes you happy if you have any questions.

Why not just link a `Protocol` to an existing `Circuit` object? Then we could easily derive the A/Z peer IPs?

```

Look at the way a `Network` is assigned to an `Interface`. It's done by way of the `Assignment` intermediate model. The `Assignment` object is what constrains the ability to assign a `Network` object to an interface. It's here where the following checks occur upon assignment:

- That the Network is a /32 or /128 (aka host address)
- That the Network is not already assigned to another `Interface` on the attached `Device`
- That the Network exists
   - If it exists, its state is set to `assigned`
   - If it doesn't exist, it is created

On an `Interface` you can look at the `addresses` assigned to it by way of `Assignment` relationships. You can also look at the `networks` that contain the `addresses`.

So I feel like this same concept could be applied or adapted for use in a `Protocol` relationship to a `Network` object by way of a `Circuit`.

```
# On the other hand, most of the benefit and power of modeling out the
# network is doing it all the way. For Z-side I also thought that we
# shouldn't require a network resource anyway because that would mean
# we're modeling potential not-our-network. (if A is on the edge)
```

I think we should consider the modeling of these objects, and perhaps this would be a case where we could allow linking of `Network` objects from other sites. As in the case where we want to track "our" networks in `site_id=1` and "their" networks in `site_id=2`.

```
+   Import Policies: List of string
+   Export Policies: List of string

# Representing route-filter, policy, route-map, etc. Should be
# interpreted to be organizational-specific
```

Why not just let these be left up to custom attributes? This seems like a super specific use-case. What about other things like md5/auth, AS path, metro, flags... etc? Perfect use-cases for attributes IMO.

You should be able to run the `curator_cli` utility as follows:

`$ curator_cli --host XXXXXXXXXXXXX.aws.found.io --port 9200 --http_auth 'XXXXXXX:XXXXXXXX' show indices`

The configuration file options can be viewed [here](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/configfile.html).
The action file options can be viewed [here](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/actionfile.html).

IMHO code like this:

    cause = ', %r' % self.info['error']['root_cause'][0]['reason']

is fairly smelly, since one might drop relevant information in the `[1:]` part of the list. Often when I use the python client, exceptions miss relevant information that I do get when reproducing the call via `curl`.

Without much knowledge of the elasticsearch-py code base, I would suggest supporting multiple REST-API version error messages like this:

    cause = ', %r % (
        self.info['error']['root_cause'][0]
        if type(self.info['error']['root_cause'][0]) == str
        else self.info['error']['root_cause'][0]['reason'])

Assuming these are the only two error formats is tricky, nevertheless not trickier than assuming the format is `error.root_cause.0.reason`.

It depends on what you mean by "restricts". Legally you can certainly call into the MPL1.0-licensed libs. However, there are people who can use BSD-licensed code who would no longer be able to use your code, i.e. your code would not actually be BSD-licensed anymore as far as downstream users are concerned. The most obvious example is that the MPL1.0 and GPL are incompatible licenses, so a GPL licensed project is allowed to use your library if it's BSD-licensed, but not if you use MPL1.0 code. Well, and there are complicated details that depend on whether they actually get distributed together or not, and possibly in this particular case they could instead choose the GPL-licensing option for FreeImage. 

I think the bottom line is, your users might or might not be okay with whatever license you end up using -- it's just that the whole (imageio + freeimage) conglomeration is not actually BSD-licensed, so instead of telling users that it it BSD you should somehow communicate what the license actually is so that they can decide whether or not it works for their particular circumstances.

This is a special case in the configurable constructor where trait assignments are explicitly done twice in order to override any values that may have been set by a classes' config. This could be resolved by moving config assignment and loading from `Configurable.__init__` to `Configurable.setup_instance` which occurs prior to `__init__`.

The way Notebook Server works we abstract the dealing with file through the "contents manager", so that the frontend is unaware that the backend is Filesystem, Postgres, GoogleDrive, DropBox, or whatever. 
Baking git in would break that assumption, and force us to deal with the case where git is not installed. 
Also why git and not mercurial. The git operation that you want to do sand support start to be complex and require lots of UI  (that even github does not provide). 

So we are staying (and will probably stay) away from adding git in our interface for a log time. 

Though,  the notebook server support extensions, and you should have all the hooks necessary in Notebook to do that. 

We'll do a basic on how to write extension at SciPy in a couple of weeks, with some written docs if you cannot attend. 

Closing as this is not something we will have in the core, but feel free to ask questions if you want to try writing an extension.

In this example, it creates `REP` socket with with `AF_SP_RAW` and stores `msg_control` which is gained from `nn_recvmsg`. After that, by using `nn_sendmsg` with stored `msg_control`, it replies to each associated `REQ` socket with out of order.

"cytoolz/curried/operator.py" should probably look a lot like (or virtually copy) that from `toolz`.  Some of the errors are due to trying to import items that don't exist in the `operator` module in different versions of Python.

Apparently a new issue has appeared in Python 2.7.10: the "keywords" attribute of `functools.partial` objects is now `{}` instead of `None` as it was in Python 2.7.9 and below, and we check that `curry` is like `partial`.  Even though TravisCI says it's using Python 2.7.9, I remain skeptical and think they are using a version that is more like 2.7.10.  Note that this isn't an issue in `toolz`, because the `toolz` implementation uses `functools.partial` under the hood.

Hey, are you trying to get predictions based directly on the resized images? I ask this because I was as surprised as you are due to low accuracy.

 If so, you are doing it wrong. AlexNet test phase is a bit different than other models. Quoting from the paper:

> At test time, the network makes a prediction by extracting five 224 × 224 patches (the four corner patches and the center patch) as well as their horizontal reflections (hence ten patches in all), and averaging the predictions made by the network’s softmax layer on the ten patches

There is also an additional step (right after above quote in the paper) that includes adding certain values obtained with eigenvectors/values to image pixels which they reported increasing test accuracy by approximately 1%.

You should achieve similar accuracy as the original work when you include above steps. Hope this helps.

Instead? I see this as routing to `foo.html` _through_ `foo.py`. `foo.py` is never directly addressable through the web.

Overnight I've come to think that we should include the renderer in the filename—`foo.html.jinja2`. This aids tooling, resolves the static/dynamic [question](https://github.com/AspenWeb/aspen.py/issues/27#issuecomment-245797343), and is parallel to how we handle `*.spt` files already: `foo.html.{spt,jinja2}` is more specific than `foo.html`. Actually, we could implement this to be flexible: files are "renderable" based on file extensions registered with Aspen, and any renderable file runs through `foo.py` (if there is one). That way, if you wanted to register `.html` instead of `.jinja2`, you could.

> less time spent switching between files.

One either switches between files, or between locations in a single file. Having the files all right next to each other in the same directory at least makes opening them all at once easier. The question is whether the benefit of having them in the same file outweighs the cost of fighting against "stupid tools and lack of standardization." There's no shortage of work in the world. After [nine years](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Slk95WWL138), I for one am ready to work on other things besides building an ecosystem around multi-mode files. ;-)

I do not think that the authenticator is the appropriate place to emit this signal.

If we take ordinary Django session authentication (e.g. Django admin), it looks like:

```
user logs in  ->  signal emitted
user access resource (page)
user access resource (page)...
user logs out
user logs in  ->  signal emitted
```

With this change, the module looks like this (unless I got it wrong):

```
user obtains token
user access resource   ->  signal emitted
user access resource   ->  signal emitted...
token expires
user obtains token
```

I would expect it to look like this:

```
user obtains token   ->  signal emitted
user access resource
user access resource
token expires
user obtains token   ->  signal emitted
```

The cnf conversions apply Tseitsin transformations and also Skolemization. Tseitsin introduces skolem constants on its own in order to represent sub-formulas. Skolemization introduces Skolem constants and Skolem functions that replace existential force quantifiers with free constants or functions. 
In both cases, you can treat Skolem functions/constants as if they are existentially quantified at the outermost or leftmost level as you say.

In general, Tseitsin transformations preserve satisfiability, and the original formula is equivalent to the existential closure of the new formula with respect to the new constants:

       F <=> exists AuxVars . tseitsin(F)

Similarly for Skolemization, which is used when the formulas contain quantifiers.
The wikipedia page on Skolemization contains relevant definitions.

Since parsing DIMACS and WCNF isn't onerous I would prefer to not expose API features for this. Below is a simple cnf to smt2 conversion script. It can be changed to directly add assertions to a solver. Z3 isn't a better solver on QBF benchmarks that have been exposed to QBF solvers, at least I hope not, because otherwise the state of QBF would be unimpressive :-). import sys def parse_decl(line, ous): decls = line.split() nvars = int(decls[2]) for i in range(nvars): ous.write("(declare-const p%d Bool)\n" % (i + 1)) def parse_clause(line, ous): literals = line.split() assert (literals[-1] == '0') ous.write("(assert (or ") for p in literals: if p == '0': break if p[0] == '-': ous.write("(not p") ous.write(p[1:]) ous.write(") ") else: ous.write("p") ous.write(p) ous.write(" ") ous.write("))\n") def parse_file(file): ins = open(file) ous = open("%s.smt2" % file, 'w') ous.write("(set-logic QF_FD)\n") line = ins.readline() parse_decl(line, ous) line = ins.readline() while line: parse_clause(line, ous) line = ins.readline() ous.write("(check-sat)") ins.close() ous.close()

The reason why I still allow a key is that RFC 7518 says this:
   The PBES2 password input is an
   octet sequence; if the password to be used is represented as a text
   string rather than an octet sequence, the UTF-8 encoding of the text
   string MUST be used as the octet sequence.

I interpret this to mean random keys composed by non utf-8 sequences are also acceptable, and does can only be represented sanely with a "oct" key.

Singularity containers support on the python side. Requires singularity installed to function (on mac I used vagrant image to test)
Permissible command lines are:
`studio run --container=/path/to/my_container.simg` -> runs the container with standard spiel about storing the artifacts and output 
`studio run --container=/path/to/my_container.simg script.py` -> runs the script.py from the current directory inside the container. The assumption is that container already has all python, system and what not dependencies preinstalled
/path/to/my_container can also be a simg:// or dockerhub:// qualified name pointing to a published container in the hub.

This has a small potential bug in it, though it's more a question of what people would expect the behavior to be.  As it's written now, if a stack has a `Default` parameter and you do not pass anything in for it, then on update, it will use that Default - rather than `UsePreviousValue`.  This means that if the template ever changes it's Default, that is what will be used, causing an update.

I think the right order of resolution for Parameters should be:

- If a parameter is passed in via the config, use that.
- If there is an existing stack and it has the Parameter already, `UsePreviousValue`
- If there is a `Default` on the Parameter, use that value.

Does that sound right to everyone? @troyready @ejholmes @aarongorka ?

tappy was designed with Python testing frameworks in mind so the internal tracking is not really exposed as public API. The closest thing to what you are looking for in tappy is the `Tracker` (https://github.com/python-tap/tappy/blob/master/tap/tracker.py) which has an API that supports `add_ok`, `add_not_ok`, and `add_skip`. I think the streaming mode would be closest to what you'd need. Something like:
```python
from tap.tracker import Tracker

tracker = Tracker(streaming=True, stream=my_open_error_file)
tracker.add_ok('SomeTestCase', 'This is the test description')
```
You may also want to consider alternative TAP projects (http://tappy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/alternatives.html) as one of them might have an API closer to what you're looking for.

Good luck!

Here is a SLIGHTLY better workaround for `trio/__init__.py` PyCharm friendliness:
```
from . import _core
from ._core import *
to_keep = [
    "TrioInternalError", "RunFinishedError", "WouldBlock", "Cancelled",
    "ResourceBusyError", "MultiError", "format_exception", "run",
    "open_nursery", "open_cancel_scope", "current_effective_deadline",
    "STATUS_IGNORED", "current_time", "current_instruments", "current_clock",
    "remove_instrument", "add_instrument", "current_statistics", "TaskLocal"
]
any(globals().__delitem__(_) for _ in _core.__dict__ if _ not in to_keep)
del _core
del to_keep
```

IMHO a better way to spawn tasks into the Trio thread would be a job submission queue that's processed by a Trio task you spawn. The advantage is that you can use your own nursery for that, allowing you to shut them all down cleanly, which is somewhat important when you want to compose your code with others.

In any case I'd investigate using trio-asyncio instead of two threads. That's why I wrote it after all. ;-)

@Traktormaster, just to ensure I understand you correctly you wish you flush all messages from higher priority queues prior to handling messages for lesser queues during high levels of production correct? 

Have you considered pausing lesser priority topics in a separate consumer group as opposed to lumping them all into one group? 

Rather than assigning and revoking partitions it may be wroth while tracking consumer lag on particular topic partitions overtime and taking action based on this. If you find that consumer lag on high priority topic partitions are growing you could simply suspend the lower priority topic partitions until you hit an EOF exception consuming the high priority topic partitions which remain in the assignment list. 

pause: https://docs.confluent.io/current/clients/confluent-kafka-python/index.html?highlight=pause#confluent_kafka.Consumer.pause

resume: https://docs.confluent.io/current/clients/confluent-kafka-python/index.html?highlight=pause#confluent_kafka.Consumer.resume

librdkafka statistics(partitions->consumer_lag specifically)
https://github.com/edenhill/librdkafka/wiki/Statistics

see stats_cb in the python docs as well: 

https://docs.confluent.io/current/clients/confluent-kafka-python/index.html?highlight=pause#configuration

Also is there a particular reason you are handling backpressure in the producer as opposed to relying on Kafka? Kafka will store the offsets up to the configured retention policy limits on the broker. Barring some resource constraint on the broker you should be able to produce with reckless abandon allowing the consumers to play catch up as necessary.

Unfortunately I've gotten side tracked from this PR since we needed a feature breaking fork to bypass this (and to reduce `acquaint_schema` calls).. It is surprisingly tricky to provide custom values with each frame of a UVC device. With UVC 1.5 spec, the closest thing to "standard" is per-frame metadata. This feature was introduced in Windows 10 (you can get some info here and for Linux, Intel is actively involved in getting this functionality up-streamed) That said, the alternatives are to embed data into pixels (F200) or expand the frame by a couple of lines (R200).

I think this is an unfortunate interaction stemming from [when .txt files were renamed to .rst](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zc.relation/commit/93f34df9ba7687a9bad8bff74141e658eec99e4d).

It all goes back to this line from setup.py:
```python
setup(
   ...
   description=text("README.rst"),
   ...
)
```

The `text` helper function inserts newlines at the end of .rst files:
```python
# simplified
def text(*args, **kwargs):
    # note: distutils explicitly disallows unicode for setup values :-/
    # http://docs.python.org/dist/meta-data.html
    tmp = []
    for a in args:
        if a.endswith('.rst'):
            f = open(os.path.join(*a.split('/')))
            tmp.append(f.read())
            f.close()
            tmp.append('\n\n')
        else:
             ...
    if len(tmp) == 1: # NEVER TRUE for .rst files, see above!
        res = tmp[0]
    else:
        res = ''.join(tmp)
    return res
```

So the description ends up being `'Index intransitive and transitive n-ary relationships.\n\n\n'`

Which translates to `PKG_INFO` as 
```
Metadata-Version: 2.1
Name: zc.relation
Version: 1.1
Summary: Index intransitive and transitive n-ary relationships.



Home-page: https://github.com/zopefoundation/zc.relation
Author: Gary Poster
Author-email: gary@zope.com
License: ZPL 2.1
Description: ================
        Relation Catalog
        ================
```

I know that wheel used the `email` libraries for [dealing with metadata](https://github.com/pypa/wheel/issues/178), leading to breakage, and I suspect PKG_INFO parsers did or do likewise, so the extra newlines after `Summary` would be interpreted as the end of the headers and beginning of the body.  The body is what gets rendered if there's no Description key, and the body isn't valid reST (unexpected indentation on line 6, `     Relation Catalog`), so...mangled page on PyPI.

Part of the Jupyter architecture is that you should not customize your kernel code based on the current frontend. Part of the reason for this is that you may have kernel code talking to several different frontends simultaneously (like a notebook and a console simultaneously). So the way to customize displays like this is to make a view message with fallback options, such as your ascii graphics.

If you're wanting to just have generic communication (i.e., messages) with javascript, and don't need the state-syncing machinery, then you might just directly use comms, which is what widgets is built on.

I looked into this further - the fact that the additional word was `u'__label__'` seemed a little suspicious, since that is also used in the input data to FastText supervised models to denote a label.

So I'm assuming the French wiki has the term `__label__` somewhere. The `threshold` method of `Dictionary` checks to see if the word begins with the term `__label__`, and if it does, it is labeled as `entry_type::label` (as opposed to `entry_type::word`). When a "word" is added, `nwords` in the dictionary is incremented, when a "label" is added, `nlabels` is incremented, `size` is incremented either way.

The actual term `__label__` is therefore marked as a label, and ignored while serializing the vectors to the `.vec` file. This is also the reason for a mismatch between `size` and `nwords` even in unsupervised models.

I'm surprised the term `__label__`  actually exists in the input training data for `wiki.fr` though.

We've written a [Python wrapper](https://github.com/RaRe-Technologies/gensim/blob/develop/gensim/models/wrappers/fasttext.py) as part of Gensim to allow users to load FastText models and use word vector functionality already present in gensim, and [this bug](https://github.com/RaRe-Technologies/gensim/issues/1236) was affecting some of our users.

Understanding timing
------------------

These calculations with naive struts take ~1.5 seconds to initialize and render, while with the pupil plane image, it takes ~60 seconds (hence my "factor of 40" comment).  I tried to understand where this was coming from, and established the following:

- The maxK and stepK for the two cases are quite similar.  So it's not like one case is handling much higher resolution images internally.
- The actual loading of the very large pupil plane file takes ~1.5 sec, so it's not like the 60 seconds is all loading images somehow.
- The ~60 seconds is nearly all in the initialization, not in the rendering.  So it's some part of the initialization but it's not the "loading the file" part that is dominating.
- For reference, the stepK and maxK for the case where we use the pupil plane image are (1.7 and 506), while a Kolmogorov with FWHM=0.65 arcsec has maxK~20.  So basically, it takes a lot of time to build a very high-res PSF and then when convolving with a typical ground-based PSF it's going to chuck out a lot of the information it has gone to great pains to calculate.

@smemsh Thanks for taking the time to file such a detailed report!

> are we using the interface incorrectly?

Yes, but I think this issue illustrates the need for better docstrings and probably a narrative use case.

The call

```python
future = subscription.open(callback)
```

has just opened a worker (actually a few workers) that will listen for messages on the `subscription` (which is a [`Policy`][3] instance). Once `open()`-ed, the `Policy` can stay open indefinitely. The **only** time that `future.result()` would be set is if the policy [gets `close()`-ed][4].

It's certainly expected that you'd call `subscription.close()` before shutting down your code that subscribes to a given topic.

So, at the end of it, the only **real** value for the future is in checking `future.done()` to make sure the workers are still running (if `done()` returns `False`) and also `future.exception()` to see if there was a failure that caused it to stop running.

Typical usage would be as in the [Pull Subscriber Guide][5]:

```python
policy = subscriber.subscribe(subscription_path)
future = policy.open(callback)

# The subscriber is non-blocking, so we must keep the main thread from
# exiting to allow it to process messages in the background.
print('Listening for messages on {}'.format(subscription_path))
while True:
    time.sleep(60)
```

i.e. your subscriber would just stay alive "forever" (hence the `time.sleep(60)`).

@zafields There is nothing to "fix" on our end, the issue is twofold:

- the [`google`][1] package puts code in the `google` package
- we use the `google` **namespace** package (see #4247 for lots of discussion)

So the usage of a non-namespace package (`google`) colliding with our packages is causing all of the issues.

I'm happy to continue the discussion about namespace packages here, but the only resolution is what is already being discussed in #4345: we need to contact the owners of the `google` package and hopefully they can do a release that uses the `google` namespace rather than a non-namespace package.

I'd also note, if you don't need the functionality in that package (`google`) just uninstall it.

> Curious to know how this code generation works so that the code in api_core/google/api_core/exceptions.py for ServiceUnavailable is used.

The generator generates wrappers for the underlying grpc client (*stub* in grpc terminology). One of the wrappers applied to all RPC methods is [`google.api_core.grpc_helpers.wrap_errors`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/e58fecd99f2f19ed5fb1026cdfd178ae3f6d8da2/api_core/google/api_core/grpc_helpers.py#L90) which handles re-mapping `grpc.RpcErrors` to appropriate exception classes. `wrap_errors` is applied to RPC methods by [`google.api_core.gapic_v1.method.wrap_method`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/api_core/google/api_core/gapic_v1/method.py#L219) which is called by the generated code ([example](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/pubsub/google/cloud/pubsub_v1/gapic/publisher_client.py#L130)).

It does help. A few comments and thoughts:

### Flow Control

> Our indexing process can lock up for a variety of reasons so we need to be able to apply backpressure to producing messages from the Pub/Sub queue

We _sort of_ have what you want here. Subscriptions have something called "flow control", which is basically a maximum number of unacked messages that they will work on. You could set the max number of messages pretty low and the client library will stop reading messages from Pub/Sub until they are dealt with. (It will not error, though.) I think it would be _mostly_ equivalent to what you have here to simply set the max messages to 10.

### asyncio

> so we pump an `asyncio.Queue` and use a configurable number of asynchronous workers to process the objects. This keeps performance up and memory usage down. We do this using `aiopg`, `aioes` and an unreleasable partial Pub/Sub client based on `aiohttp`.

> Strange encode lzo is probably something that amazon has added?

Redshift has default encodings it will apply if none are supplied, which I think is what's happening here.

```
If no compression is specified in a CREATE TABLE or ALTER TABLE statement, Amazon Redshift automatically assigns compression encoding as follows:

Columns that are defined as sort keys are assigned RAW compression.
Columns that are defined as BOOLEAN, REAL, or DOUBLE PRECISION data types are assigned RAW compression.
All other columns are assigned LZO compression.
```
